content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 10

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 10:1 -- "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" vs. "YHWH spoke to Moses and said" (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-spoke YHWH unto Moses, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH spoke to Moses and said:" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:" -- PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (violating binding convention) and renders le'mor as "and said" rather than the standard "saying." EMET uses the archaic "unto." The consensus follows LOGOS with EMET's colon after "saying."

---

### 10:2 -- "Make for yourself" / "of hammered work" / "for the summoning ... and for the setting out" vs. "for summoning ... and for signaling" (aseh lekha shtei khatsotsrot kesef miqshah ta'aseh otam ve-hayu lekha le-miqra ha-edah u-le-massa ha-makhanot)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Make for-yourself two trumpets of silver; of-hammered-work you-shall-make them; and-they-shall-be for-you for-the-summoning of the-congregation and-for-the-setting-out of the-camps." |
| LOGOS | "Make for yourself two trumpets of silver; of hammered work you shall make them. And they shall be for you for the summoning of the congregation and for the setting out of the camps." |
| PNEUMA | "Make two trumpets of silver -- you are to make them from hammered work. They will serve you for summoning the congregation and for signaling the camps to set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Make for yourself two trumpets of silver; of hammered work you shall make them. And they shall be for you for the summoning of the congregation and for the setting out of the camps." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "for yourself" (lekha), a significant omission since the Hebrew specifies the personal address. (b) PNEUMA restructures "of hammered work you shall make them" into "you are to make them from hammered work," reversing the Hebrew clause order where miqshah is fronted for emphasis. (c) PNEUMA renders ve-hayu lekha as "They will serve you," an interpretive paraphrase of the simple "and they shall be for you." (d) PNEUMA renders u-le-massa ha-makhanot as "for signaling the camps to set out," where the Hebrew simply says "for the setting out of the camps" -- "signaling" is an interpretive addition. (e) PNEUMA uses dashes instead of semicolons, altering the structural feel. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:3 -- "they blow on both of them" vs. "they blow on them" vs. "both trumpets are blown" / "shall assemble" vs. "shall gather themselves" vs. "is to assemble" / "Tent of Meeting" vs. "tent of meeting" (ve-taqe'u ba-hennah ve-no'adu elekha kol ha-edah el petakh Ohel Mo'ed)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-shall-blow on-them, and-shall-assemble unto-you all the-congregation unto the-entrance of the-Tent of Meeting." |
| LOGOS | "And when they blow on both of them, all the congregation shall gather themselves to you at the entrance of the tent of meeting." |
| PNEUMA | "When both trumpets are blown, the entire congregation is to assemble before you at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when they blow on both of them, all the congregation shall assemble to you at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). (b) EMET translates ba-hennah as simply "on them"; LOGOS and PNEUMA both specify "both" to contrast with v. 4's "one," which is implicit in the Hebrew dual suffix. The consensus follows LOGOS's explicit "both of them." (c) PNEUMA uses passive voice "are blown" where the Hebrew has active taqe'u ("they shall blow"). (d) PNEUMA uses "entire" for kol; "all" is more literal. (e) PNEUMA uses "before you" for elekha; "to you" is more precise for the Hebrew el + suffix. (f) LOGOS uses lowercase "tent of meeting"; the consensus capitalizes "Tent of Meeting" per established convention. (g) EMET uses archaic "unto" (twice). (h) The verb: EMET/PNEUMA use "assemble"; LOGOS uses "gather themselves" (reflecting the niphal no'adu, "assemble/meet together"). "Assemble" suffices for the niphal sense.

---

### 10:4 -- "on one" vs. "on one" vs. "only one trumpet is blown" / "leaders" vs. "leaders" vs. "leaders -- the heads of the clans" / "thousands" vs. "clans" (be-akhat yitqa'u ve-no'adu elekha ha-nesi'im rashei alfei Yisra'el)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-if on-one they-blow, and-shall-assemble unto-you the-leaders, the-heads of the-thousands of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And if they blow on one, then the leaders, the heads of the thousands of Israel, shall gather themselves to you." |
| PNEUMA | "If only one trumpet is blown, then the leaders -- the heads of the clans of Israel -- are to assemble before you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if they blow on one, then the leaders, the heads of the thousands of Israel, shall assemble to you." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening "And" and adds "only" and "trumpet" (not in MT; the Hebrew says simply be-akhat, "on one"). (b) PNEUMA uses passive "is blown" where the Hebrew has active yitqa'u. (c) PNEUMA renders alfei as "clans" rather than "thousands." While elef can mean either "thousand" or "clan/military unit," the consensus preserves the traditional "thousands" following EMET/LOGOS, since the Hebrew is ambiguous and "thousands" preserves the ambiguity for the reader. (d) PNEUMA uses em-dashes for the appositive where EMET/LOGOS use commas. (e) PNEUMA uses "before you" for elekha; EMET uses "unto you"; LOGOS uses "to you." The consensus follows LOGOS's "to you."

---

### 10:5 -- "blow an alarm" vs. "sound a war-blast" / "encamped on the east side" vs. "encamping eastward" vs. "stationed on the east side" (u-teqa'tem teru'ah ve-nas'u ha-makhanot ha-khonim qedmah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-you-shall-blow an-alarm, and-shall-journey the-camps the-ones-encamping eastward." |
| LOGOS | "And when you blow an alarm, the camps that are encamped on the east side shall set out." |
| PNEUMA | "When you sound a war-blast, the camps stationed on the east side are to set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when you blow an alarm, the camps that are encamped on the east side shall set out." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention). (b) PNEUMA renders teru'ah as "war-blast," an interpretive expansion; while teru'ah does have military associations, the basic meaning is "alarm/shout," and "alarm" preserves the Hebrew range of meaning without importing the specific "war" connotation. EMET/LOGOS both use "alarm." (c) PNEUMA uses "stationed" for ha-khonim ("the ones encamping"), a military paraphrase of the Hebrew participle from khanah ("to encamp"). LOGOS's "that are encamped" is closer. (d) EMET uses "eastward" (qedmah literally); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "on the east side." The consensus follows LOGOS's "on the east side" for English clarity. (e) PNEUMA uses "are to set out" (instructional) vs. EMET/LOGOS's "shall set out" (legislative). The consensus uses "shall."

---

### 10:6 -- "blow an alarm a second time" vs. "sound a second war-blast" / "An alarm shall be blown for their setting out" vs. "for their journeyings" vs. "whenever they are to set out" (u-teqa'tem teru'ah shenit ... teru'ah yitqe'u le-mas'eihem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-you-shall-blow an-alarm a-second-time, and-shall-journey the-camps the-ones-encamping southward; an-alarm they-shall-blow for-their-journeyings." |
| LOGOS | "And when you blow an alarm a second time, the camps that are encamped on the south side shall set out. An alarm shall be blown for their setting out." |
| PNEUMA | "When you sound a second war-blast, the camps stationed on the south side are to set out. A war-blast is to be sounded whenever they are to set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when you blow an alarm a second time, the camps that are encamped on the south side shall set out. An alarm shall be blown for their setting out." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention) and again uses "war-blast" for teru'ah (see v. 5). (b) PNEUMA adds "whenever" to le-mas'eihem, an interpretive expansion; the Hebrew simply says "for their journeyings/settings out." (c) EMET renders le-mas'eihem as "for their journeyings"; LOGOS uses "for their setting out." Both are valid; "setting out" better matches the context of departure signals. (d) PNEUMA uses "stationed" and "are to set out" as in v. 5. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:7 -- "But when assembling the assembly" vs. "And-in-the-assembling the-assembly" / "you shall blow, but you shall not sound an alarm" vs. "blow a long, steady note -- not the war-blast" (u-ve-haqhil et ha-qahal titqe'u ve-lo tari'u)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-in-the-assembling the-assembly, you-shall-blow and-not you-shall-sound-an-alarm." |
| LOGOS | "But when assembling the assembly, you shall blow, but you shall not sound an alarm." |
| PNEUMA | "But when assembling the congregation, you are to blow a long, steady note -- not the war-blast." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But when assembling the assembly, you shall blow, but you shall not sound an alarm." -- (a) PNEUMA adds "a long, steady note" -- this interpretive expansion is not in the Hebrew, which says simply titqe'u ("you shall blow"). The acoustic description, while historically accurate, is PNEUMA's extrapolation from the teru'ah/teqi'ah distinction. (b) PNEUMA uses "not the war-blast" with an em-dash, replacing the Hebrew's explicit negative command ve-lo tari'u ("and you shall not sound an alarm"). (c) PNEUMA renders ha-qahal as "the congregation" rather than "the assembly"; the Hebrew qahal ("assembly") is distinct from edah ("congregation") in v. 3, and the consensus preserves this distinction. (d) EMET's "And-in-the-assembling" preserves the Hebrew u-ve-haqhil; LOGOS's "But" captures the contrastive sense. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:8 -- "as a perpetual statute throughout your generations" vs. "for-a-statute of perpetuity for-your-generations" vs. "a permanent statute for you throughout your generations" (le-khuqqat olam le-doroteikhem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-sons of Aaron, the-priests, they-shall-blow on-the-trumpets; and-they-shall-be for-you for-a-statute of perpetuity for-your-generations." |
| LOGOS | "And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow on the trumpets; and they shall be for you as a perpetual statute throughout your generations." |
| PNEUMA | "The sons of Aaron, the priests, are to blow the trumpets. This is to be a permanent statute for you throughout your generations." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow on the trumpets; and they shall be for you as a perpetual statute throughout your generations." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" at the start and "on" before "the trumpets" (the Hebrew has ba-khatsotsrot, "on/with the trumpets"). (b) PNEUMA splits into two sentences and uses "This is to be" for ve-hayu lakhem, losing the continuity of the single Hebrew sentence. (c) PNEUMA uses "permanent" for olam; "perpetual" better captures the Hebrew olam ("forever/in perpetuity"). (d) EMET's "for-a-statute of perpetuity" is hyper-literal; LOGOS's "as a perpetual statute" is smoother. (e) EMET's "for-your-generations" vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "throughout your generations" -- "throughout" better renders the Hebrew le- in this distributive sense. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:9 -- "go to war" vs. "go to war" / "the adversary who oppresses you" vs. "an enemy who oppresses you" / "you shall be saved" vs. "you will be delivered" (ve-khi tavo'u milkhamah be-artsekhem al ha-tsar ha-tsorer etkhem ... ve-nosha'tem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-when you-shall-go to-war in-your-land against the-adversary the-one-oppressing you, and-you-shall-sound-an-alarm on-the-trumpets, and-you-shall-be-remembered before YHWH your-God, and-you-shall-be-saved from your-enemies." |
| LOGOS | "And when you go to war in your land against the adversary who oppresses you, then you shall sound an alarm with the trumpets, and you shall be remembered before YHWH your God, and you shall be saved from your enemies." |
| PNEUMA | "When you go to war in your own land against an enemy who oppresses you, you are to sound the war-blast on the trumpets. Then you will be remembered before YHWH your God, and you will be delivered from your enemies." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when you go to war in your land against the adversary who oppresses you, then you shall sound an alarm on the trumpets, and you shall be remembered before YHWH your God, and you shall be saved from your enemies." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention) and adds "own" to "your land" (not in MT). (b) PNEUMA uses "an enemy" for ha-tsar; the Hebrew has the definite article (ha-tsar, "the adversary"), which EMET/LOGOS preserve. (c) PNEUMA again uses "war-blast" for the alarm. (d) PNEUMA uses "you will be delivered" for ve-nosha'tem; EMET/LOGOS use "you shall be saved." The niphal of yasha' ("to save") is best rendered "saved" -- "delivered" imports a slightly different semantic range. (e) PNEUMA splits into two sentences, breaking the Hebrew's chain of ve-qatal clauses. (f) EMET uses "the-one-oppressing" (participial); LOGOS uses "who oppresses" (relative clause); PNEUMA uses "who oppresses." The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:10 -- "on the day of your gladness" vs. "On your days of rejoicing" / "appointed feasts" vs. "appointed festivals" / "beginnings of your months" vs. "new moon celebrations" / "peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offerings" / "a memorial" vs. "a reminder" (u-ve-yom simkhatkhem u-ve-mo'adeikhem u-ve-rashei khodsheikhem ... zivkhei shalmeikhem ... le-zikkaron)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-in-the-day of your-gladness and-in-your-appointed-feasts and-in-the-beginnings of your-months, and-you-shall-blow on-the-trumpets over your-burnt-offerings and-over the-sacrifices of your-peace-offerings; and-they-shall-be for-you for-a-memorial before your-God; I YHWH your-God." |
| LOGOS | "And on the day of your gladness, and at your appointed feasts, and at the beginnings of your months, you shall blow on the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings; and they shall be for you a memorial before your God. I am YHWH your God." |
| PNEUMA | "On your days of rejoicing -- your appointed festivals and your new moon celebrations -- you are to blow the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over your fellowship offerings. They will serve as a reminder of you before your God. I am YHWH your God." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And on the day of your gladness, and at your appointed feasts, and at the beginnings of your months, you shall blow on the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings; and they shall be for you as a memorial before your God. I am YHWH your God." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" and restructures the three liturgical occasions into "days of rejoicing" (plural, where MT has singular yom) with the other two as appositives in em-dashes, collapsing the Hebrew's three distinct parallel phrases. (b) PNEUMA uses "rejoicing" for simkhah; "gladness" is closer to the Hebrew. (c) PNEUMA uses "festivals" for mo'adim; "feasts" is the traditional rendering. (d) PNEUMA uses "new moon celebrations" for rashei khodsheikhem ("beginnings of your months"), an interpretive paraphrase. (e) PNEUMA uses "fellowship offerings" for shalmim; "peace offerings" is the traditional rendering of shelamim/shalmim. (f) PNEUMA omits "the sacrifices of" before "your peace/fellowship offerings" (the MT has zivkhei shalmeikhem, "sacrifices of your peace offerings"). (g) PNEUMA uses "a reminder of you" for le-zikkaron; "a memorial" is more formal and preserves the technical cultic term. (h) EMET omits "am" in the closing formula; LOGOS/PNEUMA supply it for English grammar. The consensus follows LOGOS with the addition of "as" before "a memorial."

---

### 10:11 -- "it came to pass" vs. no introductory formula / "the cloud was taken up" vs. "went up" vs. "the cloud lifted" / "the Tabernacle of the Testimony" vs. "the tabernacle of the testimony" (va-yehi ... na'alah he-anan me-al Mishkan ha-Edut)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-was in-the-year the-second, in-the-month the-second, on-the-twentieth in-the-month, went-up the-cloud from-upon the-Tabernacle of the-Testimony." |
| LOGOS | "And it came to pass in the second year, in the second month, on the twentieth day of the month, the cloud was taken up from over the tabernacle of the testimony." |
| PNEUMA | "In the second year, on the twentieth day of the second month, the cloud lifted from above the Tabernacle of the Testimony." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it came to pass in the second year, in the second month, on the twentieth day of the month, the cloud was taken up from over the Tabernacle of the Testimony." -- (a) PNEUMA omits the opening va-yehi formula ("And it came to pass") and "And." (b) EMET uses hyper-literal word order "in-the-year the-second." (c) The cloud: EMET's "went up" is the most literal for na'alah (qal of alah); LOGOS's "was taken up" implies passive/divine agency (as if hophal); PNEUMA's "lifted" is idiomatic. The qal na'alah is intransitive ("went up"), but "was taken up" has precedent in English Bible tradition and conveys the same sense. The consensus follows LOGOS. (d) PNEUMA reorders the date clauses, placing "on the twentieth day of the second month" together, departing from the MT's order (year, then month, then day-in-month). (e) LOGOS uses lowercase "tabernacle of the testimony"; the consensus capitalizes per established convention.

---

### 10:12 -- "set out on their journeys" vs. "set out on their journeys" vs. "set out on their journeys" / "settled" vs. "settled" vs. "came to rest" (va-yis'u ... le-mas'eihem ... va-yishkon he-anan be-midbar Pa'ran)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-journeyed the-Sons of Israel for-their-journeyings from-the-wilderness of Sinai; and-dwelt the-cloud in-the-wilderness of Paran." |
| LOGOS | "And the Sons of Israel set out on their journeys from the wilderness of Sinai, and the cloud settled in the wilderness of Paran." |
| PNEUMA | "And the Sons of Israel set out on their journeys from the wilderness of Sinai. The cloud came to rest in the wilderness of Paran." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the Sons of Israel set out on their journeys from the wilderness of Sinai, and the cloud settled in the wilderness of Paran." -- (a) EMET uses "journeyed" for va-yis'u and "dwelt" for va-yishkon; LOGOS uses "set out" and "settled"; PNEUMA uses "set out" and "came to rest." The verb shakan means "to dwell/settle/tabernacle"; "settled" captures this well. (b) PNEUMA splits into two sentences, breaking the Hebrew's vav-consecutive chain. (c) EMET's "for-their-journeyings" is hyper-literal; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "on their journeys" is clearer. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:13 -- "at first" vs. "for the first time" vs. "at the mouth of YHWH" vs. "according to YHWH's command" (va-yis'u ba-rishonah al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-journeyed at-the-first upon the-mouth of YHWH by-the-hand of Moses." |
| LOGOS | "And they set out at first at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses." |
| PNEUMA | "They set out for the first time according to YHWH's command through Moses." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out for the first time at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" (binding convention). (b) ba-rishonah: EMET "at the first"; LOGOS "at first"; PNEUMA "for the first time." PNEUMA's rendering makes the inaugural sense explicit, which is contextually appropriate. The consensus follows PNEUMA's "for the first time." (c) al pi YHWH: EMET/LOGOS preserve "at/upon the mouth of YHWH" -- the anthropomorphic Hebrew idiom. PNEUMA renders as "according to YHWH's command," an interpretive paraphrase that loses the vivid Hebrew metaphor. The consensus preserves "at the mouth of YHWH." (d) be-yad Mosheh: EMET/LOGOS preserve "by the hand of Moses"; PNEUMA uses "through Moses," losing the embodied Hebrew idiom. The consensus preserves "by the hand of Moses."

---

### 10:14 -- "standard of the camp" vs. "division under the banner of the camp" / "according to their armies" vs. "company by company" / "Nahshon the son of" vs. "Nahshon son of" (degel makhaneh benei Yehudah ... le-tsiv'otam ... Nakhshon ben Amminadav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-journeyed the-standard of the-camp of the-sons of Judah at-the-first by-their-hosts; and-over its-host Nahshon son of Amminadab." |
| LOGOS | "And the standard of the camp of the sons of Judah set out first, according to their armies, and over its army was Nahshon the son of Amminadab." |
| PNEUMA | "The division under the banner of the camp of Judah set out first, company by company. Over its army was Nahshon son of Amminadab." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the standard of the camp of the sons of Judah set out first, according to their armies, and over its army was Nahshon the son of Amminadab." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" and adds "The division under the banner of" for degel, an interpretive expansion; degel simply means "standard/banner." (b) PNEUMA omits "the sons of" before "Judah" (the MT has benei Yehudah). (c) PNEUMA renders le-tsiv'otam as "company by company" rather than "according to their armies/hosts." While idiomatic, this loses the military term tsava ("army/host"). (d) EMET uses "hosts" for tsiv'otam; LOGOS uses "armies." Both are valid; "armies" is clearer in English. (e) PNEUMA splits into two sentences. (f) "the son of" vs. "son of" -- LOGOS includes the article; EMET/PNEUMA omit it. The Hebrew ben lacks an article, but English convention for names traditionally includes "the." The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:15 -- "the sons of Issachar" vs. "the Sons of Issachar" (matteh benei Yissakhar)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-over the-host of the-tribe of the-sons of Issachar Nethanel son of Zuar." |
| LOGOS | "And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Issachar was Nethanel the son of Zuar." |
| PNEUMA | "Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Issachar was Nethanel son of Zuar." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Issachar was Nethanel the son of Zuar." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And." (b) EMET uses "host" for tseva; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "army." (c) PNEUMA capitalizes "Sons" in "Sons of Issachar" -- "Sons of Israel" is capitalized per convention, but individual tribal references use lowercase "sons of." (d) EMET/PNEUMA omit "the" before "son of"; LOGOS includes it. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:16 -- Same pattern as v. 15 (minor conjunction and article differences)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-over the-host of the-tribe of the-sons of Zebulun Eliab son of Helon." |
| LOGOS | "And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Zebulun was Eliab the son of Helon." |
| PNEUMA | "Over the army of the tribe of the Sons of Zebulun was Eliab son of Helon." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Zebulun was Eliab the son of Helon." -- Same issues as v. 15. PNEUMA omits "And" and capitalizes "Sons." The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:17 -- "And the Tabernacle was taken down" vs. "Then the Tabernacle was taken down" / "bearing the Tabernacle" vs. "carrying the Tabernacle" (ve-hurad ha-Mishkan ve-nas'u benei Gershon u-venei Merari nos'ei ha-Mishkan)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-was-taken-down the-Tabernacle, and-journeyed the-sons of Gershon and-the-sons of Merari, bearers of the-Tabernacle." |
| LOGOS | "And the tabernacle was taken down, and the sons of Gershon and the sons of Merari set out, bearing the tabernacle." |
| PNEUMA | "Then the Tabernacle was taken down, and the Sons of Gershon and the Sons of Merari set out, carrying the Tabernacle." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the Tabernacle was taken down, and the sons of Gershon and the sons of Merari set out, bearing the Tabernacle." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "Then" instead of "And" for the conjunction. (b) PNEUMA capitalizes "Sons of Gershon" and "Sons of Merari" -- these are tribal/clan designations, not the "Sons of Israel" convention. (c) PNEUMA uses "carrying" for nos'ei; EMET/LOGOS use "bearing." Both are valid; "bearing" has a more formal register. (d) LOGOS uses lowercase "tabernacle"; the consensus capitalizes per convention. The consensus follows LOGOS with capitalization.

---

### 10:18 -- "standard of the camp of Reuben" vs. "division under the banner of the camp of Reuben" / "according to their armies" vs. "company by company" (degel makhaneh Re'uven le-tsiv'otam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-journeyed the-standard of the-camp of Reuben by-their-hosts; and-over its-host Elizur son of Shedeur." |
| LOGOS | "And the standard of the camp of Reuben set out, according to their armies, and over its army was Elizur the son of Shedeur." |
| PNEUMA | "Next the division under the banner of the camp of Reuben set out, company by company. Over its army was Elizur son of Shedeur." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the standard of the camp of Reuben set out, according to their armies, and over its army was Elizur the son of Shedeur." -- Same pattern as v. 14: PNEUMA uses "Next" instead of "And," expands degel to "the division under the banner," uses "company by company" for le-tsiv'otam, and splits into two sentences. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:19 -- Same pattern as vv. 15–16

### 10:21 -- "bearing the holy things" vs. "bearing the sanctuary" vs. "carrying the holy objects" / "the Tabernacle was set up before their coming" vs. "before they arrived" (ha-Qehatim nos'ei ha-miqdash ve-heqimu et ha-Mishkan ad bo'am)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-journeyed the-Kohathites, bearers of the-sanctuary; and-they-set-up the-Tabernacle until their-coming." |
| LOGOS | "And the Kohathites set out, bearing the holy things; and the tabernacle was set up before their coming." |
| PNEUMA | "Then the Kohathites set out, carrying the holy objects. The Tabernacle would be set up before they arrived." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the Kohathites set out, bearing the holy things; and the Tabernacle was set up before their coming." -- (a) ha-miqdash: EMET's "the sanctuary" is a straightforward rendering; LOGOS's "the holy things" and PNEUMA's "the holy objects" both interpret miqdash as referring to the sacred vessels rather than the sanctuary structure. In context, the Kohathites carry the sacred objects (per Num 4:4–15), so "the holy things" is contextually appropriate and follows the LXX's ta hēgiusmena ("the sanctified things"). (b) PNEUMA uses "Then" instead of "And" and "carrying" instead of "bearing." (c) EMET's "until their coming" for ad bo'am is hyper-literal; "before their coming" (LOGOS) captures the temporal sense in English. (d) PNEUMA uses "would be set up" (conditional) and "before they arrived" (past tense), departing from the narrative tense. (e) PNEUMA splits into two sentences. The consensus follows LOGOS with capitalized "Tabernacle."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the standard of the camp of the sons of Ephraim set out, according to their armies, and over its army was Elishama the son of Ammihud." -- Same pattern as vv. 14, 18. PNEUMA uses "Next," expands degel, omits "the sons of," uses "company by company," and splits sentences. The consensus follows LOGOS.

### 10:25 -- "the rearguard for all the camps" vs. "serving as the rear guard for all the camps" / "according to their armies" vs. "company by company" (me'assef le-khol ha-makhanot)

| LOGOS | "And the standard of the camp of the sons of Dan set out, the rearguard for all the camps, according to their armies, and over its army was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai." |
| PNEUMA | "Then the division under the banner of the camp of Dan set out -- serving as the rear guard for all the camps -- company by company. Over its army was Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the standard of the camp of the sons of Dan set out, the rearguard for all the camps, according to their armies, and over its army was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "Then" instead of "And," expands degel, omits "the sons of," uses "serving as the rear guard" (adding "serving as" not in MT), uses em-dashes, and splits into two sentences. (b) EMET uses "of-all" where LOGOS/PNEUMA use "for all"; the Hebrew le-khol uses le- ("for"). (c) "Rearguard" (EMET/LOGOS, one word) vs. "rear guard" (PNEUMA, two words) -- both are acceptable; the consensus uses the single-word form. The consensus follows LOGOS.

### 10:28 -- "These were the journeyings" vs. "These were the order of march" vs. "This was the marching order" / "according to their armies" vs. "division by division" (eleh mas'ei benei Yisra'el le-tsiv'otam va-yissa'u)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "These the-journeyings of the-Sons of Israel by-their-hosts; and-they-journeyed." |
| LOGOS | "These were the order of march of the Sons of Israel according to their armies. And they set out." |
| PNEUMA | "This was the marching order of the Sons of Israel, division by division, when they set out." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "These were the journeyings of the Sons of Israel according to their armies. And they set out." -- (a) mas'ei: EMET's "journeyings" is the literal plural of massa ("journey/setting out"); LOGOS's "order of march" and PNEUMA's "marching order" are both interpretive paraphrases. "Journeyings" preserves the Hebrew noun. (b) PNEUMA uses "This" (singular) where the Hebrew has eleh ("these," plural). (c) PNEUMA uses "division by division" for le-tsiv'otam; see notes on previous verses. (d) PNEUMA combines the last clause "when they set out," merging va-yissa'u into the preceding sentence; the MT has it as a separate clause: "and they set out." (e) EMET omits "were" and uses "by-their-hosts." The consensus combines EMET's literal "journeyings" with LOGOS's structure.

### 10:29 -- "We are setting out" vs. "Journeying we" / "Come with us" vs. "Come" / "we will do good to you" vs. "we will treat you well" / "good things concerning Israel" vs. "good concerning Israel" (nos'im anakhnu ... lekhah ittanu ve-hetavnu lakh ... dibber tov al Yisra'el)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-said Moses to-Hobab son of Reuel the-Midianite, father-in-law of Moses: 'Journeying we unto the-place which YHWH said, "It I-shall-give to-you." Come with-us and-we-shall-do-good to-you, for YHWH has-spoken good concerning Israel.'" |
| LOGOS | "And Moses said to Hobab the son of Reuel the Midianite, the father-in-law of Moses, 'We are setting out for the place of which YHWH said, "I will give it to you." Come with us and we will do good to you, for YHWH has spoken good concerning Israel.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Moses said to Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite, Moses' father-in-law, 'We are setting out for the place of which YHWH said, "I will give it to you." Come with us and we will treat you well, for YHWH has spoken good things concerning Israel.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses said to Hobab the son of Reuel the Midianite, the father-in-law of Moses, 'We are setting out for the place of which YHWH said, "I will give it to you." Come with us and we will do good to you, for YHWH has spoken good concerning Israel.'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And." (b) EMET uses "Journeying we" (hyper-literal for nos'im anakhnu); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "We are setting out." (c) PNEUMA renders ve-hetavnu lakh as "we will treat you well" -- an idiomatic paraphrase of the more literal "we will do good to you" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew heitiv means "to make good/do good," and "do good to you" preserves the root. (d) PNEUMA adds "things" to "good things concerning Israel"; the MT simply says tov ("good"). (e) PNEUMA uses "Moses' father-in-law" (possessive) rather than "the father-in-law of Moses" (LOGOS). (f) EMET/PNEUMA use "son of" without article; LOGOS uses "the son of." The consensus follows LOGOS.

### 10:30 -- "I will not go, but rather to my own land" vs. "Not I-shall-go; but unto my-land" vs. "I will not go. Instead, I will return" / "my own kindred" vs. "my kindred" vs. "my own kindred" (lo elekh ki im el artsi ve-el moladeti elekh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-said unto-him: 'Not I-shall-go; but unto my-land and-unto my-kindred I-shall-go.'" |
| LOGOS | "And he said to him, 'I will not go, but rather to my own land and to my own kindred I will go.'" |
| PNEUMA | "But Hobab said to him, 'I will not go. Instead, I will return to my own land and to my own kindred.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And he said to him, 'I will not go, but rather to my own land and to my own kindred I will go.'" -- (a) PNEUMA uses "But Hobab said" -- adding the name "Hobab" (not in MT, which simply has va-yomer, "and he said") and using "But" instead of "And." (b) PNEUMA uses "Instead" for ki im ("but rather/except"). (c) PNEUMA adds "return" -- a significant interpretive addition; the Hebrew simply repeats elekh ("I will go"), not "I will return" (ashuv). (d) PNEUMA restructures the sentence, moving "I will go" to the beginning and losing the emphatic Hebrew word order where the destinations are fronted: "to my land and to my kindred I will go." (e) EMET uses "unto" (archaic). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:31 -- "Please do not leave us" vs. "Please do not forsake us" / "for you know our encamping" vs. "for you know where we should camp" / "you shall be for us as eyes" vs. "you can serve as eyes for us" (al na ta'azov otanu ki al ken yada'ta khanotenu ba-midbar ve-hayita lanu le-einayim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-said: 'Please, do-not forsake us, because on-account-of you-know our-encamping in-the-wilderness, and-you-shall-be for-us for-eyes.'" |
| LOGOS | "And he said, 'Please do not leave us, for you know our encamping in the wilderness, and you shall be for us as eyes.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Moses said, 'Please do not leave us, for you know where we should camp in the wilderness, and you can serve as eyes for us.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And he said, 'Please do not leave us, for you know our encamping in the wilderness, and you shall be for us as eyes.'" -- (a) PNEUMA adds "Moses" (the MT has va-yomer, "and he said," without naming the speaker). (b) EMET uses "forsake" for ta'azov; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "leave." The Hebrew azav means "to leave/abandon/forsake"; "leave" is sufficient and less theologically charged than "forsake." (c) EMET uses "on-account-of" for al ken; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "for" -- the clause provides the reason for the request, and "for" captures this. (d) PNEUMA expands khanotenu ("our encamping") to "where we should camp," adding "where" and "should" not in the Hebrew. (e) PNEUMA uses "you can serve as eyes for us" for ve-hayita lanu le-einayim; LOGOS preserves the Hebrew structure "you shall be for us as eyes." PNEUMA's "can serve as" imports ability/possibility not present in the Hebrew vav-consecutive perfect ("and you shall be"). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:32 -- "whatever good YHWH does for us" vs. "the good ... which YHWH shall do good with us" / "we will do the same for you" (ve-hayah ki telekh immanu ve-hayah ha-tov ha-hu asher yeitiv YHWH immanu ve-hetavnu lakh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-shall-be, when you-shall-go with-us, and-it-shall-be -- the-good the-that which YHWH shall-do-good with-us, and-we-shall-do-good to-you." |
| LOGOS | "And it shall be, if you go with us, then whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you." |
| PNEUMA | "If you come with us, then whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it shall be, if you go with us, then whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And it shall be" (ve-hayah, the opening formula). (b) PNEUMA uses "come" for telekh ("go/walk"); "go" is more literal. (c) EMET preserves the Hebrew's repetitive structure (ve-hayah ... ve-hayah, "and it shall be ... and it shall be"), which is characteristic of Hebrew conditional syntax. LOGOS/PNEUMA streamline to a single conditional. (d) EMET's "and-we-shall-do-good to-you" preserves the Hebrew ve-hetavnu lakh; LOGOS/PNEUMA paraphrase as "we will do the same for you." LOGOS's rendering is clearer in English while preserving the essential meaning. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:33 -- "they set out" vs. "they journeyed" vs. "So they set out" / "the ark of the covenant of YHWH went before them" vs. "was journeying before them" / "to seek out" vs. "to spy out" (va-yis'u me-har YHWH derekh sheloshet yamim va-Aron berit YHWH nose'a lifneihem ... la-tur lahem menukhah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-journeyed from-the-mountain of YHWH a journey of three days; and-the-Ark of the-Covenant of YHWH journeying before-them a journey of three days to-seek-out for-them a-resting-place." |
| LOGOS | "And they set out from the mountain of YHWH a journey of three days, and the ark of the covenant of YHWH went before them a journey of three days, to seek out a resting place for them." |
| PNEUMA | "So they set out from the mountain of YHWH on a journey of three days, with the ark of the covenant of YHWH traveling ahead of them for those three days to seek out a resting place for them." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they set out from the mountain of YHWH a journey of three days, and the ark of the covenant of YHWH went before them a journey of three days, to seek out a resting place for them." -- (a) PNEUMA uses "So" instead of "And." (b) PNEUMA adds "on" before "a journey" and "with" before "the ark," restructuring the Hebrew's paratactic syntax (vav-consecutive) into a prepositional phrase. (c) PNEUMA uses "traveling ahead of them" for nose'a lifneihem; "went before them" (LOGOS) is closer to the Hebrew while preserving clarity. (d) PNEUMA uses "for those three days" instead of the Hebrew's repeated "a journey of three days" (derekh sheloshet yamim), losing the deliberate repetition. (e) EMET uses the present participle "journeying" for nose'a; LOGOS's "went" and PNEUMA's "traveling" differ. The consensus uses "went" (LOGOS) for narrative flow. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:34 -- "the cloud of YHWH was over them by day when they set out" vs. "by day in their journeying" vs. "by day whenever they set out" (va-anan YHWH aleihem yomam be-nos'am min ha-makhaneh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-cloud of YHWH upon-them by-day in-their-journeying from the-camp." |
| LOGOS | "And the cloud of YHWH was over them by day, when they set out from the camp." |
| PNEUMA | "The cloud of YHWH was over them by day whenever they set out from camp." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the cloud of YHWH was over them by day when they set out from the camp." -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And" and "the" before "camp." (b) PNEUMA uses "whenever" for be-nos'am, importing iterative/habitual aspect; the Hebrew infinitive construct be-nos'am ("in their setting out") does not explicitly mark iteration. "When" (LOGOS) preserves the temporal sense without adding frequency. (c) EMET lacks the verb "was," producing a fragment ("the cloud of YHWH upon them"); LOGOS/PNEUMA supply it for English grammar. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 10:35 -- "Arise, O YHWH" vs. "Rise up, YHWH!" / "let your enemies be scattered" vs. "let those who hate you flee before you" vs. "let those who hate you flee before you!" (qumah YHWH ve-yafutsu oyevekha ve-yanusu mesan'ekha mi-panekha)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-was in-the-journeying of the-Ark, and-said Moses: 'Arise, YHWH, and-let-be-scattered your-enemies, and-let-flee the-ones-hating-you from-before-you.'" |
| LOGOS | "And it came to pass, when the ark set out, that Moses said, 'Arise, O YHWH, and let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you.'" |
| PNEUMA | "Whenever the ark set out, Moses would say, 'Rise up, YHWH! Let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it came to pass, when the ark set out, that Moses said, 'Arise, O YHWH, and let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you!'" -- (a) PNEUMA omits "And it came to pass" (va-yehi, the narrative formula). (b) PNEUMA uses "Whenever ... would say" (iterative/habitual), while the Hebrew va-yehi bi-nso'a ... va-yomer is a simple narrative sequence ("and it was when ... set out, and ... said"). The iterative reading is an interpretation (possibly correct theologically, but not grammatically required by the vav-consecutive). (c) PNEUMA uses "Rise up" for qumah; EMET/LOGOS use "Arise." Both are valid; "Arise" has the stronger liturgical register in English. (d) LOGOS adds the vocative "O" before YHWH; EMET does not. The vocative "O" is appropriate in English for the imperative address and is included in the consensus. (e) EMET uses "the-ones-hating-you" (participial, hyper-literal) for mesan'ekha; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "those who hate you." (f) PNEUMA adds exclamation marks for dramatic effect. The consensus retains one exclamation mark at the end. The consensus follows LOGOS with an exclamation mark at the close.

---

### 10:36 -- "And when it rested, he said" vs. "And-in-its-resting, he-would-say" vs. "And when it came to rest, he would say" / "Return, O YHWH, to the ten thousands of the thousands of Israel" vs. "the myriads of the thousands" vs. "the countless thousands" (u-ve-nukhoh yomar shuvah YHWH rivevot alfei Yisra'el)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-in-its-resting, he-would-say: 'Return, YHWH, the-myriads of the-thousands of Israel.'" |
| LOGOS | "And when it rested, he said, 'Return, O YHWH, to the ten thousands of the thousands of Israel.'" |
| PNEUMA | "And when it came to rest, he would say, 'Return, YHWH, to the countless thousands of Israel!'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And when it rested, he said, 'Return, O YHWH, to the ten thousands of the thousands of Israel.'" -- (a) rivevot alfei: EMET renders "the myriads of the thousands"; LOGOS renders "the ten thousands of the thousands"; PNEUMA renders "the countless thousands." The Hebrew rivevot means "myriads/ten-thousands" and alfei means "thousands." PNEUMA's "countless thousands" collapses the two distinct numerical terms into a single phrase and imports "countless" (not in Hebrew). EMET's "myriads" is accurate but less accessible. LOGOS's "the ten thousands of the thousands" preserves both Hebrew terms and follows the binding convention of English-word numbers. (b) PNEUMA uses "he would say" (iterative); EMET uses "he-would-say" (also iterative, following the Hebrew yiqtol yomar); LOGOS uses "he said." The Hebrew yomar is imperfect, which could be iterative ("he would say") or simple past in sequence. Given the pairing with v. 35 (which uses va-yomer, simple past), the consensus follows LOGOS's "he said" for consistency within the verse pair. (c) LOGOS adds vocative "O" before YHWH; the consensus retains this for liturgical register. (d) PNEUMA adds "came to" before "rest" and adds an exclamation mark. (e) EMET omits "to" before the myriads -- the Hebrew lacks an explicit preposition, but the sense requires the implied directional "to." LOGOS supplies it. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Patterns

**Systematic PNEUMA divergences:** Throughout Numbers 10, PNEUMA consistently (1) omits the opening "And" from verses, violating the binding convention for preserving conjunctions; (2) uses "war-blast" for teru'ah instead of the more literal "alarm"; (3) renders le-tsiv'otam as "company by company" instead of "according to their armies"; (4) expands degel to "the division under the banner" rather than the simpler "the standard"; (5) capitalizes "Sons" in tribal references (e.g., "Sons of Gershon") where only "Sons of Israel" should be capitalized per convention; (6) uses sequential markers ("Then," "Next," "So") instead of the Hebrew conjunction vav rendered as "And"; (7) tends toward iterative/habitual readings ("whenever," "would say") where the Hebrew may be read as simple narrative.

**Systematic EMET divergences:** EMET consistently (1) uses archaic "unto" for el/al; (2) hyphenates compound phrases; (3) uses "host(s)" for tsava/tsiv'ot where "army/armies" is the consensus; (4) uses participial constructions ("the-ones-encamping," "the-ones-hating-you") where relative clauses are clearer in English.

**LOGOS as baseline:** In this chapter, LOGOS most frequently provided the consensus reading, with its formal register, preserved conjunctions, and balanced literalism. EMET's contribution was primarily in cases where LOGOS had strayed from the Hebrew structure, and PNEUMA's contribution was primarily in clarifying ba-rishonah as "for the first time" (v. 13).

**Verses with full or near-full agreement:** No verse showed complete three-way agreement due to PNEUMA's systematic omission of "And" and structural differences. The marching-order verses (15–16, 19–20, 23–24, 26–27) showed the closest agreement in substance, with differences limited to the conjunction, article, and capitalization patterns noted above.
